original_abstract: Single-pixel imaging offers a transformative approach to capturing scenes without a direct line of sight, yet achieving high image fidelity remains difficult, particularly in environments with significant ambient noise. Here, we demonstrate a robust single-pixel imaging method that reconstructs high-quality images by systematically acquiring their Fourier spectrum. Our approach utilizes four-step phase-shifting sinusoidal structured illumination to sample specific spatial frequencies. By applying an inverse Fourier transform to the resulting spectrum, we can faithfully reconstruct the target scene. Because this technique relies on the integrated light collection rather than spatial resolution at the detector, it allows for flexible sensor placement, enabling imaging even when the detector is positioned around corners or behind diffusers. Furthermore, the strategy exploits the inherent sparsity of natural images in the Fourier domain, allowing for accurate image reconstruction from sub-Nyquist measurements. Experimental results confirm that this method effectively suppresses environmental noise and eliminates the direct-current component, producing clear, high-resolution images even under challenging illumination conditions where traditional single-pixel methods typically struggle.
humanized_abstract: Single-pixel imaging has attracted increasing attention in the last few years for imaging around obstacles or through opaque media. However, achieving a high image quality is a significant challenge, especially in the presence of strong background noise. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective single-pixel imaging method to obtain high-quality images of a scene by sequentially obtaining the Fourier transform of the image of the scene. To achieve this, we use four-step phase-shifting sinusoidal structured illumination to record the desired spatial frequencies. An inverse Fourier transform is then applied to the spectrum to provide the image of the scene. Unlike existing single-pixel imaging approaches, our method only relies on the integrated intensity of the collected light and not on the spatial resolution provided by the detector, thus allowing flexibility in the position of the sensor and allowing the detector to be located around corners or behind diffusers and other opaque media. In addition, the proposed method takes advantage of the fact that most natural images have a sparse Fourier transform, which allows for high-quality image reconstruction using sub-Nyquist measurements. Several experiments have been carried out to validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, demonstrating that the proposed approach significantly reduces the impact of background noise and eliminates the DC component of the image, allowing high-quality images to be obtained. This is demonstrated with other single-pixel imaging methods that are unable to obtain a useful image in strong background noise environments such as the white LED lighting background.
